title: Idioms in Work, Education, and Social Life
learningObjectives: Learn idioms used in professional and educational settings
Understand idioms about success, failure, and effort
Practice recognizing idioms in longer texts
Use idioms appropriately in intermediate-level conversations

sections.title: Idioms for Intermediate Learners
sections.content: At the B1 level, you encounter idioms in more complex situations — at work, in education, and in social discussions. These idioms help you express ideas about effort, success, challenges, and relationships. Mastering them will significantly improve your fluency.

sections.title: Idioms at Work
sections.content:
The business world is full of idioms:

• Get the ball rolling - Start something. <Let's get the ball rolling on this project.>
• Think outside the box - Be creative. <We need to think outside the box to solve this.>
• Go the extra mile - Do more than expected. <She always goes the extra mile for her clients.>
• Cut corners - Do something cheaply or quickly, sacrificing quality. <Don't cut corners on safety.>
• Back to square one - Start again from the beginning. <The plan failed, so we're back to square one.>
• On the same page - In agreement. <Let's make sure we're all on the same page.>

sections.title: Idioms in Education
sections.content:
Students and teachers use many idioms:

• Learn the ropes - Learn how to do something new. <It took me a semester to learn the ropes at university.>
• Pass with flying colors - Pass easily with excellent results. <She passed the exam with flying colors.>
• Burn the midnight oil - Study or work very late. <I burned the midnight oil to finish my thesis.>
• A steep learning curve - Something difficult to learn at first. <Programming has a steep learning curve.>
• Hit the nail on the head - Say exactly the right thing. <Your analysis hit the nail on the head.>

sections.title: Idioms in Context
sections.content:
Example 1: <The new curriculum is challenging, but the students are learning the ropes quickly.> (curriculum = plan of study)

Example 2: <After three semesters of hard work, she graduated with flying colors and got a scholarship for her tuition.> (tuition = university fees)

Example 3: <The renewable energy project hit a wall, but the team didn't give up. They went back to square one and came up with a better plan.>

sections.title: Idioms in Social Situations
sections.content:
These idioms are commonly used in social conversations:

• <Break the ice> - Do something to make people feel more comfortable in a new situation
• <Hit it off> - Immediately get along well with someone
• <On the same wavelength> - Think in a similar way to someone
• <Rub someone the wrong way> - Annoy or irritate someone
• <Bend over backwards> - Try very hard to help someone
• <Bite your tongue> - Stop yourself from saying something

Example: <At the conference, I didn't know anyone, so I tried to break the ice by asking about their work. I hit it off with a woman from Brazil — we were completely on the same wavelength about education reform.>

sections.title: Idioms About Money and Success
sections.content:
Money and success idioms appear frequently in everyday English:

• <Cost an arm and a leg> - Be very expensive
• <Break the bank> - Spend more money than you can afford
• <Make ends meet> - Earn just enough money to live
• <Hit the jackpot> - Have great luck or success
• <Go from rags to riches> - Become wealthy after being poor
• <Cut corners> - Do something cheaply or with less effort

Example: <The new apartment costs an arm and a leg, but it's in a great location. We're trying to make ends meet by cooking at home instead of eating out. My uncle went from rags to riches — he started with nothing and now owns three restaurants.>

sections.title: Idioms in Real Situations
sections.content:
Example 1: JOB INTERVIEW - <I was so nervous that I almost bit my tongue when they asked about my weaknesses. But I think I hit it off with the interviewer — we were on the same wavelength.>

Example 2: SHOPPING - <This designer bag costs an arm and a leg! I don't want to break the bank. Let's look for something more affordable.>

Example 3: HELPING A FRIEND - <My neighbour bent over backwards to help me move house. She even drove me to the furniture shop. I'm so grateful.>

sections.title: Key Takeaways
sections.content:
Remember:
• Work idioms help you sound professional in meetings and emails
• Education idioms are useful for discussing academic experiences
• Many idioms use visual images — picture them to remember better
• Practice using idioms in the right context
• Don't overuse idioms — one or two per conversation is natural

keyVocabulary.definition: To start an activity or process
keyVocabulary.example: Let's get the ball rolling on the new project.
We need to think outside the box.

keyVocabulary.definition: To make more effort than expected
keyVocabulary.example: Good employees go the extra mile.

keyVocabulary.definition: Return to the beginning after failure
keyVocabulary.example: The experiment failed — back to square one.
He passed the test with flying colors.

keyVocabulary.definition: To work or study very late at night
keyVocabulary.example: Students burn the midnight oil before exams.

keyVocabulary.term: hit the nail on the head
keyVocabulary.definition: To describe exactly what is causing a situation
keyVocabulary.example: Your comment hit the nail on the head.

keyVocabulary.definition: To learn how a particular task or job is done
keyVocabulary.example: It takes time to learn the ropes.

grammarPoints.rule: Idioms in Formal vs Informal Contexts
grammarPoints.explanation: Some idioms are appropriate for both formal and informal situations, while others are more casual. <Think outside the box> works in a business meeting, but <hit the books> is more casual.
examples: Formal: 'Let's get the ball rolling on this initiative.'
Informal: 'I need to hit the books tonight.'
commonMistakes: Using very casual idioms in formal writing: 'The CEO needs to hit the books' sounds odd in a report
